Name: information-theoretic_compression_bounds
Title: Information-Theoretic Compression Bounds for Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the fundamental bit-cost of preserving task-relevant information in neural networks can be characterized by a rate-distortion function, and practical compression methods can be evaluated against this theoretical limit.
Related Work: Recent work in mixed-precision quantization (e.g., EMQ, SNIP) and information-theoretic bounds (e.g., Bottlenecks CLUB) has explored efficient compression and theoretical limits. However, these approaches either focus on empirical proxies or lack a unified framework connecting practical methods to fundamental limits. Our proposal bridges this gap by directly linking neural network compression to rate-distortion theory, providing a theoretical foundation for evaluating compression methods.
Abstract: This paper proposes a novel framework for characterizing the fundamental bit-cost of preserving task-relevant information in neural networks using rate-distortion theory. We derive achievable bounds on the bits-per-weight required to maintain predictive accuracy under a given distortion constraint. Our approach provides a unified theoretical foundation for evaluating practical compression methods, such as quantization and pruning, against these fundamental limits. We introduce estimators for task-relevant information and distortion measures, enabling empirical validation of our bounds. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our framework can guide the design of compression methods, achieving near-optimal performance while maintaining task accuracy. Our results offer new insights into the trade-offs between compression efficiency and predictive sufficiency, advancing the understanding of neural network compressibility.

Experiments.Description: Derive achievable bounds on bits-per-weight for various neural network architectures using rate-distortion theory.
Experiments.Metrics: Bits-per-weight, distortion measure (e.g., mean squared error)

Experiments.Description: Implement estimators for task-relevant information and validate their accuracy on benchmark datasets.
Experiments.Metrics: Mutual information estimates, correlation with task accuracy

Experiments.Description: Evaluate practical compression methods (e.g., quantization, pruning) against the derived bounds.
Experiments.Metrics: Compression ratio, task accuracy, distortion measure

Experiments.Description: Conduct ablation studies to analyze the impact of different distortion measures on compression performance.

Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical bounds may be too conservative for practical applications.
Estimators for task-relevant information may introduce bias or variance.
The choice of distortion measure may not capture all aspects of task-relevant information.
The framework may not generalize well to all neural network architectures or tasks.
